threat_description: Bitcoin ECDSA transaction hijacking: P2PK (Pay-to-Public-Key) outputs in Bitcoin expose the secp256k1 public key directly on-chain — these UTXOs are immediately quantum-vulnerable via Shor's algorithm. P2PKH and P2WPKH addresses initially expose only the public-key hash but become quantum-vulnerable after their first spend (when the public key is revealed in the signature). The combined quantum-exposed surface includes early Satoshi-era P2PK coins and any address that has ever transacted.
crypto_at_risk: secp256k1 ECDSA, P2PK UTXOs (immediate quantum exposure), P2PKH/P2WPKH addresses post-first-spend
pqc_replacement: P2QRH (BIP-360) proposal with ML-DSA and SLH-DSA

Original conflated these.

threat_description: Blockchain quantum vulnerability: Ethereum uses ECDSA secp256k1 for transaction signing. Ethereum addresses are keccak256-hashes of the public key — the public key is exposed once an account broadcasts its first outbound transaction (revealed in the ECDSA signature), creating direct Shor-attack exposure for any address that has ever transacted. The Ethereum Foundation roadmap discusses account abstraction (EIP-4337, EIP-7560) as a path toward quantum-safe wallets, but no finalized EIP mandates a PQ migration timeline.
crypto_at_risk: ECDSA secp256k1 transaction signing, public-key exposure on first outbound transaction, BIP-32/39/44 HD wallet derivation
pqc_replacement: Account Abstraction (EIP-4337), Verkle Trees, quantum-resistant signature schemes

Quantum exposure is per-account, post-first-transaction.

Constrained sensors lack crypto-agility for mid-life PQC migration. Quantum-enabled data integrity attacks could inject false sensor readings causing treatment failures and public health emergencies.
crypto_at_risk: RSA/ECC sensor-to-gateway key bootstrap, TLS for cloud data aggregation, IoT provisioning certificates
pqc_replacement: Post-quantum key agreement for sensor provisioning, lightweight PQC for constrained IoT, hybrid gateway encryption

threat_description: Wastewater treatment SCADA quantum vulnerability: Wastewater SCADA systems are architecturally distinct from drinking water treatment serving sewage pumping biosolids handling and effluent discharge control. EPA/CISA guidance (August 2024) classifies wastewater under essential services. Newer systems use OPC UA and DNP3 Secure Authentication with RSA/ECC key establishment. Quantum-enabled decryption of control commands could corrupt treatment processes releasing untreated sewage into waterways with environmental and public health catastrophe.
crypto_at_risk: RSA/ECC in OPC UA certificates, DNP3 Secure Authentication, VPN for remote HMI access
pqc_replacement: Quantum-resistant OPC UA profiles, post-quantum DNP3 authentication, hybrid VPN for wastewater SCADA

threat_description: Critical infrastructure ICS/SCADA quantum exposure: Industrial control systems managing power grids, water treatment, and pipelines use legacy cryptographic protocols. Many SCADA systems still use Modbus, DNP3, and older OPC protocols without native encryption. Where encryption exists, RSA and ECDSA are commonly used for authentication; IEC 62443 defines security levels and zone/conduit architecture, delegating algorithm specifics to IEC 62351 and vendor implementations. OPC-UA security uses RSA/ECDSA for application authentication.
crypto_at_risk: All asymmetric cryptography across 16 CI sectors, sector-specific SCADA/ICS protocols, federal PKI
pqc_replacement: Sector-specific PQC migration roadmaps, FIPS 203/204/205 compliance, CISA ACDI deployment

Removed NERC CIP misattribution (moved to CI-003 pipeline coverage).

threat_description: Power grid protection relay authentication: IEC 61850 standard defines communication protocols for electrical substation automation. IEC 62351-6 specifies symmetric HMAC-SHA-256 for GOOSE and Sampled Values message authentication — the sub-4ms latency requirement of GOOSE messages precludes per-message asymmetric signing. RSA/ECDSA is used in IEC 62351-8 (Role-Based Access Control) and the station-level TLS/PKI hierarchy for session establishment and certificate-based authentication.
crypto_at_risk: IEC 62351-8 RBAC RSA/ECDSA, station TLS certificates, IEC 62351-6 HMAC keys (symmetric — distributed via quantum-vulnerable PKI), protection relay PKI
pqc_replacement: ML-KEM/ML-DSA per ECCG ACM v2.0, hybrid implementations, PQC-by-default for new systems by 2030
data_quality_notes: Proposed 2026-05-06: source_url_quality flag added; trusted_source_id_status tagged (etsi and w3c now resolved via proposed trusted_sources). 2026-05-18 Pass-2 fix: IEC 62351-6 originally specified RSA for GOOSE/SV; HMAC-SHA-256 was added in later revisions because RSA couldn't meet latency. Both options exist. 2026-05-18 accuracy fix: Major correction: GOOSE/SV use HMAC (IEC 62351-6) not RSA. Quantum risk is in PKI key distribution and IEC 62351-8 RBAC layer.

TSA Security Directives (SD-02C series) and PHMSA regulations (49 CFR Part 192/195) govern pipeline cybersecurity in the US. Pipeline Management System security relies on TLS with RSA/ECDSA for operator authentication.

pqc_replacement: Crypto-agile OPC UA with ML-KEM/ML-DSA, quantum-resistant VPN overlays for Modbus, post-quantum DNP3 SA

threat_description: NIST algorithm deprecation timeline: NIST IR 8547 (Transition to Post-Quantum Cryptography Standards, IPD November 2024) establishes the deprecation trajectory for quantum-vulnerable public-key algorithms — RSA-2048/ECDSA-P-256/ECDH-P-256 expected to be disallowed for US federal use post-2030/2035. NIST SP 800-131A Rev 2 governs key-length transitions for currently approved algorithms (a different scope). Organizations must complete PQC migration before classical algorithm prohibition.
crypto_at_risk: AES-128, 3DES (112-bit effective), RC4, DES, legacy symmetric ciphers with key sizes < 256 bits
pqc_replacement: AES-256, ChaCha20-Poly1305, migration to 256-bit symmetric keys for all long-term data confidentiality

Updated main_source to Rev 5. 2026-05-18 accuracy fix: Reordered citations: IR 8547 is the primary PQC transition reference; SP 800-131A is key-length transitions for currently approved algos.

threat_description: Hash function collision resistance halved by quantum algorithms: Grover's algorithm reduces the pre-image resistance of SHA-256 from 256-bit to ~128-bit effective security. Quantum collision attacks (Brassard-Høyer-Tapp / BHT) reduce SHA-256 collision resistance from 128-bit to ~85-bit effective security. SHA-1 (already classically broken) is fully negligible post-CRQC. Quantum security of a hash for collision/preimage is determined by output size: SHA-384, SHA-512, SHA3-384, and SHA3-512 (larger outputs) provide substantially improved quantum margins; SHA3-256 has the same 256-bit output as SHA-256 and therefore equivalent quantum resistance. HMAC-SHA-256 is less affected due to keying.
crypto_at_risk: SHA-1 (broken classically), SHA-256 collision resistance, MD5, RIPEMD-160, Merkle trees using 256-bit hashes
pqc_replacement: SHA-384, SHA-512, SHA3-256, SHA3-512 for collision-sensitive applications; SLH-DSA hash chains use SHA-256/SHA-512 by design
main_source: NIST SP 800-57 Part 1 Rev 5 + NIST IR 8105
data_quality_notes: Proposed 2026-05-06: source_url_quality flag added; trusted_source_id_status tagged (etsi and w3c now resolved via proposed trusted_sources). 2026-05-19 Pass-4 fix: SP 800-57 Part 1 Rev 5 (not Rev 6 — doesn't exist). Replaced IR 8545 (PQC Round 4 status report, not hash guidance) with IR 8105 (Report on PQC). Corrected SHA-3 claim — SHA3-256 has same quantum resistance as SHA-256 (output-size determined).

threat_description: NIST RNG quantum considerations: NIST SP 800-90A Rev 1 (2015) defines approved DRBGs — CTR_DRBG, Hash_DRBG, and HMAC_DRBG (Dual_EC_DRBG was removed in Rev 1 following the 2013 backdoor concerns). HMAC-DRBG and CTR-DRBG configured with 256-bit security retain ~128-bit effective quantum security under Grover's algorithm, well above any practical CRQC threshold. RNG quantum risk is largely mitigated by adequate symmetric security parameters.
crypto_at_risk: DRBG implementations using <256-bit symmetric parameters; entropy source quality (independent of quantum)
pqc_replacement: NIST SP 800-90A DRBG (CTR_DRBG with AES-256), NIST SP 800-90B validated hardware entropy, QRNG for HSM seeding
data_quality_notes: Proposed 2026-05-06: source_url_quality flag added; trusted_source_id_status tagged (etsi and w3c now resolved via proposed trusted_sources). 2026-05-18 accuracy fix: Reframed: Dual_EC removal is historical (2015); properly parameterized HMAC/CTR-DRBG are quantum-resistant. RNG is a smaller quantum risk than RSA/ECDSA.

threat_description: Side-channel attacks on post-quantum implementations: ML-KEM, ML-DSA, and SLH-DSA software implementations that are not constant-time are vulnerable to timing analysis, cache-timing attacks, and power side-channels that can expose private key material without breaking the underlying mathematical hardness assumption. FALCON (FN-DSA) requires Gaussian sampling with tight timing constraints especially vulnerable to FPA cache timing. FIPS 140-3 Derived Test Requirements require constant-time implementations for all sensitive cryptographic operations. Production deployments must use validated implementations (OpenSSL 3.5+, AWS-LC, BoringSSL) or hardware accelerators with demonstrated side-channel resistance.
crypto_at_risk: Naive ML-KEM/ML-DSA implementations without constant-time guarantees, FN-DSA Gaussian sampler timing leakage, SLH-DSA WOTS+ chain computation timing
pqc_replacement: Constant-time PQC implementations per FIPS 140-3 DTR, FIPS 140-3 Level 3+ HSMs, hardware accelerators (NPCM, Arm CryptoCell-713 with PQC extensions)
data_quality_notes: Proposed 2026-05-06: source_url_quality flag added; trusted_source_id_status tagged (etsi and w3c now resolved via proposed trusted_sources).

threat_description: Stateful hash-based signatures for IoT: NIST SP 800-208 (XMSS/LMS/HSS) provides quantum-resistant signatures suitable for IoT firmware updates where private key state can be maintained. LMS/HSS is preferred for one-time firmware signing scenarios. NIST IoT cybersecurity guidance lives in SP 800-213 and the NISTIR 8259 series; stateful hash-based signatures are an applicable migration path for the firmware-signing subset of IoT use cases.
crypto_at_risk: RSA/ECDSA firmware signing keys, IoT code-signing PKI, OTA update authentication, long-lived embedded device signing roots
pqc_replacement: Use ML-KEM-1024/ML-DSA-87 for highest-value data; monitor NIST IR 8545 updates and NIST PQC standardization announcements; maintain cryptographic agility for algorithm migration

Correct IoT refs are SP 800-213 and NISTIR 8259 series.

threat_description: Fault injection attacks on PQC hardware implementations: Hardware Security Modules implementing ML-KEM and ML-DSA are susceptible to differential fault analysis (DFA) and laser fault injection (LFI) attacks that deliberately induce computation errors to leak secret key material. FN-DSA (FALCON) is particularly sensitive: a single fault during NTRU key generation can expose the secret polynomial. FIPS 140-3 Level 3 requires physical tamper resistance and active response mechanisms. Level 4 requires full environmental protection. Current production HSMs (Thales Luna, Entrust nShield, Utimaco) with PQC support must demonstrate fault countermeasures including redundant computation, error detection codes, and physical tamper response per FIPS 140-3 requirements.
crypto_at_risk: ML-KEM private key generation in HSMs without fault countermeasures, FN-DSA (FALCON) secret polynomial, ML-DSA private key during signing without redundant computation
pqc_replacement: FIPS 140-3 Level 3+ HSMs with validated PQC implementations, physical tamper response, redundant computation verification, and CMVP-validated algorithm testing
main_source: NIST FIPS 140-3 Physical Security Requirements / IACR PQC fault attack research
data_quality_notes: Proposed 2026-05-06: source_url_quality flag added; trusted_source_id_status tagged (etsi and w3c now resolved via proposed trusted_sources).

SOCI Act requires critical infrastructure entities to implement and maintain critical infrastructure risk management programs. Telcos operating national carrier-grade infrastructure must protect communications under RSA/ECDSA certificates.

pqc_replacement: ML-KEM-1024 for IPsec/TLS key exchange, ML-DSA-87 for BGP route authentication; SOCI Act critical infrastructure obligations require 2030 compliance

threat_description: AI-assisted cryptanalysis acceleration: Research published 2024–2025 explores whether machine learning heuristics can guide lattice reduction algorithms (BKZ block selection, basis pruning) and automate side-channel analysis against ML-KEM and ML-DSA implementations. No published peer-reviewed result has demonstrated a meaningful reduction in security against standardized ML-KEM/ML-DSA parameter sets, but the active research direction warrants ongoing monitoring of cryptanalytic frontier.
crypto_at_risk: Lattice-based primitives (ML-KEM, ML-DSA) under research surveillance; non-constant-time implementations vulnerable to ML-aided side-channel attack
pqc_replacement: ML-KEM-768/1024 (higher security margins), constant-time implementations, hardware PQC accelerators

threat_description: PKCS#11 v3.2 PQC mechanism gaps: PKCS#11 v3.2 (OASIS Committee Specification Draft 01, April 2025) adds ML-KEM and ML-DSA mechanisms; it is not yet an approved OASIS Standard pending member ballot. The currently approved OASIS Standard remains PKCS#11 v3.1. Legacy PKCS#11 v2.40 deployments cannot support PQC algorithms without middleware updates. HSM vendors must certify new firmware implementing PKCS#11 v3.2 mechanisms before customers can migrate.
crypto_at_risk: HSM-backed RSA/ECDSA keys awaiting PKCS#11 v3.2 firmware upgrade paths, PKCS#11 v2.40 deployments unable to expose ML-KEM/ML-DSA mechanisms
pqc_replacement: HSM firmware upgrade to PKCS#11 v3.2 with PQC buffer support; hardware replacement if firmware upgrade not available
